summary: I enlisted in the United States Army in November 1965 after graduating from Niles West High School. I wanted to gain a skill, so I chose to be a cook with the MOS 94B20. My basic training was at Fort Knox, Kentucky, followed by quartermaster school at Fort Lee, Virginia. I was deployed to Vietnam during the war and served as a quartermaster supporting a Hawk missile site.

I graduated from Niles West High School in 1965, where I was involved in athletics like football, basketball, and baseball. I also took Home Economics, which helped prepare me for my Army role as a cook. My high school sweetheart, Linda Friedman, was also from the area, and we got engaged during my 30-day leave from the Army before I shipped out to Vietnam.

My basic training in the United States Army took place at Fort Knox, Kentucky. It was my first time away from home for any length of time, and I adjusted by relying on my faith and attending church services on base. Fort Knox was where I began my military journey before moving on to quartermaster school.

After basic training, I went to Fort Lee, Virginia, for a 12-week quartermaster school. There, I learned about sanitation, cooking in the field, baking, and food storage. It was a valuable experience that gave me practical skills, and I even served as an altar boy at the Catholic church on the base.

I was deployed to Vietnam during the Vietnam War after completing my quartermaster training. I served at Cam Ranh Bay and Nha Trang, supporting Hawk missile sites that protected key ports and Special Forces. The experience was challenging but rewarding, and I even got to take R&R in Hong Kong during the conflict.

Linda Friedman was my high school sweetheart from Lincolnwood, Illinois. We got engaged during my 30-day leave from the Army before I went to Vietnam, and we've been married for 50 years now. She graduated from Niles West High School and was a big part of my life before and after my military service.

Linda Friedman, my future wife, lived in Lincolnwood, Illinois while we were dating. It was part of the greater Chicago area where I grew up and went to school. Lincolnwood was a familiar place connected to my personal life during my Army years.

As a kid, I always wanted to go to West Point because of my family's military tradition. However, I struggled with a learning disability and was a slow learner, so it wasn't a realistic option for me. Instead, I found my path in the Army as a quartermaster cook, which suited my skills better.

When I was deployed to Vietnam, I flew out of San Francisco on Continental Airlines. It was my second time on a plane, and the journey included stops in Hawaii, Clark Air Base in the Philippines, and finally Tan Son Nhut Air Base in Saigon. This flight marked the beginning of my overseas service.

San Francisco was the city where I boarded my flight to Vietnam on Continental Airlines. It was a major departure point for soldiers heading overseas during the war. From there, I made stops in Hawaii and the Philippines before arriving in Vietnam.

Hawaii was the first stop on my flight to Vietnam after leaving San Francisco. It was a brief but important layover on my journey overseas. The warm climate there was a prelude to the even warmer weather I would experience in Vietnam.

Clark Air Base in the Philippines was another stop on my flight to Vietnam. It was a key U.S. military installation in the Pacific during the Vietnam War. From Clark Air Base, I continued on to Tan Son Nhut Air Base in Saigon to begin my service in-country.

Tan Son Nhut Air Base in Saigon was where I arrived and processed when I got to Vietnam. It was a major hub for U.S. forces during the war. After processing there, I was flown by C-130 to Cam Ranh Bay to join my unit.

Saigon was the city where I first landed in Vietnam at Tan Son Nhut Air Base. The heat was intense, and it took some time to adjust to the climate. Saigon was the gateway to my deployment and the place where I began my service in-country.

After processing in Saigon, I was transported by a C-130 Hercules plane to Cam Ranh Bay. It was my first experience flying on a military cargo plane, and it took me closer to my assigned unit. The C-130 was essential for moving troops and supplies around Vietnam.

Cam Ranh Bay was a large port and headquarters for the 71st Artillery Regiment where I was initially stationed in Vietnam. It was a major logistics hub with Navy presence as well. I was only there a few days before being sent to Nha Trang, but it was an important part of my deployment.

I was assigned to the 71st Artillery Regiment in Vietnam, which operated Hawk missile sites to protect key ports like Cam Ranh Bay and Nha Trang. Although I was part of an artillery unit, my role was as a quartermaster cook supporting the soldiers. The regiment played a critical role in air defense during the war.

I was stationed at Nha Trang at Camp McDermott, where we had a Hawk missile site protecting the air base and Special Forces. We lived in tents in what was called Tent City, and I was responsible for preparing food for both the base camp and the missile site personnel. Nha Trang was a smaller but strategically important location.

Camp McDermott at Nha Trang was where I was based in Vietnam. We lived in tents and operated a Hawk missile site to defend against air attacks. My job was to prepare and deliver food to the soldiers both at the base and up at the missile site on the hill.

The Hawk missile was the air defense system our artillery unit operated in Vietnam. We protected ports like Cam Ranh Bay and Nha Trang from potential air attacks. Although I was not directly involved in firing the missiles, I supported the missile crews by providing food and supplies.

Our base at Nha Trang was located on a beachhead with the China Sea behind us. The location was beautiful but also challenging due to the tropical climate and mosquitoes. We used mosquito nets to protect ourselves while sleeping in tents near the sea.

The PX on the base was a place where we could buy snacks, toiletries, and even gamble a little. It helped boost morale by providing entertainment options like movies and slot machines. The PX was a welcome respite from the hardships of military life in Vietnam.

The Morton Grove Women's Club adopted me and my platoon while I was in Vietnam. There were several boys from Morton Grove serving there, and they sent us care packages with goodies like cookies and toiletries. The local newspaper, the Morton Grove Champion, ran articles about me and my service.

The Morton Grove Champion was the local newspaper that featured articles about me while I was serving in Vietnam. They covered my adoption by the Morton Grove Women's Club and my role in the Army. It was nice to have that connection to home through the paper.

While in Vietnam, I was able to keep in touch with my family and girlfriend through ham radio operators. They helped facilitate communication by relaying messages, which was a real blessing given the limited options we had. It was a unique way to stay connected despite the distance and war.

During my R&R in February, I went to Hong Kong for the New Year's celebration. I chose Hong Kong because I wanted to get a tuxedo custom-made for my upcoming wedding. It was a memorable trip during a difficult time in Vietnam.

After returning from Vietnam, I was stationed at Fort Lewis in Washington state for a year. My wife and I honeymooned across the country after our wedding, which happened shortly after she graduated from Niles West. Fort Lewis was my first post-war assignment back in the U.S.

Although Bob Hope was not directly mentioned in my story, I know he was famous for entertaining troops during the Vietnam War. His USO shows were a big morale booster for soldiers like me stationed overseas. I appreciated all efforts to keep spirits up during tough times.
